title: Daily care to protect eyesight
excerpt: Ayurveda has kept eyes sharp for centuries with habits as simple as oiling your feet at night. Here is the classical view on daily eye care, straight from the granthas.
body_html:
when your eyes feel the cost of the day

By evening, most of us notice it: a dull ache behind the eyes, a little dryness, that gritty feeling after hours of a screen or fluorescent light. We reach for eye drops or splash cold water and carry on. Ayurveda names this accumulated strain Netra Shrama, tiredness of the eyes, and says it builds quietly until the tissues of vision, the netra dhatu, begin to lose their nourishment.

The classical physicians took eyesight very seriously. Sushruta, writing in the Sushruta Samhita (Uttaratantra section on netra roga), lists the eye as among the most precious of the sense organs, rooted in the Pitta dosha and dependent on the continuous flow of ojas, the body's finest vital essence. Protecting sight, they argued, is not an act you perform once at a clinic; it is a daily discipline woven into ordinary life.

the classical view: eyes, pitta, and the feet

Ayurveda places the eyes under the governance of Alochaka Pitta, the sub-dosha responsible for vision and the perception of light and colour (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). When Pitta rises through excess heat, late nights, spicy food, long hours of fine-focus work, or emotional stress, it can disturb Alochaka Pitta and begin to dry or inflame the delicate structures of the eye.

One of the most consistently recommended practices across the granthas is Padabhyanga, the nightly oiling of the feet. It may seem unrelated to the eyes, but the classical texts explain that certain nadis (channels) connect the soles of the feet directly to the head and eyes (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, on the benefits of Abhyanga). Massaging warm sesame or coconut oil into the soles before sleep is said to calm Vata and Pitta simultaneously, helping the eyes rest and recover through the night.

Amla, the Indian gooseberry known as Amalaki, holds a special place in eye care throughout the tradition. The Bhavaprakasha Nighantu describes Amalaki as the foremost of Rasayana herbs: cooling, rich in natural vitamin C equivalents, and deeply nourishing to the tissues. A small spoonful of amla powder or a glass of fresh amla juice in the morning is among the simplest things you can do for long-term visual health.

triphala, anjana, and other everyday practices

Triphala, the three-fruit combination of Amalaki, Bibhitaki, and Haritaki, is specifically recommended for washing the eyes in both the Ashtanga Hridaya (Uttarasthana, the section on eye disorders) and the Sushruta Samhita. The practice is straightforward: steep a level teaspoon of Triphala powder in a cup of water overnight, strain it carefully through a fine cloth or coffee filter in the morning until no powder remains, then use the clear liquid to wash the eyes with a clean eye cup. The wash is mildly astringent and cooling, and regular use is said to strengthen the eye tissues over time.

The tradition of Anjana, lining the inner rim of the eye with medicated collyrium or natural kajal, is described at length in the Sushruta Samhita (Uttaratantra) and the Ashtanga Hridaya. Classical kajal prepared from pure lamp soot, ghee, and camphor was intended not just for cosmetic effect but to protect the delicate conjunctiva from dust, wind, and excess Pitta heat. If you do use kajal, choose preparations without synthetic chemicals, and follow the classical habit of washing the eyes clean before applying fresh kajal each morning.

Avoiding direct heat to the eye region is another point the granthas stress repeatedly. Working close to open flames, staring into bright sun without pause, and washing your face with very hot water are all considered harmful to Alochaka Pitta. The Bhavaprakasha recommends looking gently at the early morning sky before sunrise and at bodies of calm water as naturally cooling for the eyes, simple enough to build into a morning routine.

when to see a doctor or vaidya

The habits described here are general wellness practices drawn from classical Ayurvedic sources. They are not a diagnosis and are not a substitute for medical care. If you notice sudden changes in your vision, pain inside the eye, flashes of light, floaters that appear suddenly, redness that does not clear in a day or two, or any injury to the eye, please see a qualified ophthalmologist without delay. These can be signs of conditions such as glaucoma, retinal problems, or infection that need prompt professional attention. Similarly, if you have diabetes, high blood pressure, or a family history of eye disease, regular eye check-ups with a doctor are essential, regardless of any supportive home practices you follow. A qualified Ayurvedic vaidya can also assess your constitution and recommend practices specific to your body type.

Try This Today

  • Triphala eye wash: Steep one level teaspoon of Triphala powder in a cup of room-temperature water overnight. In the morning, strain twice through a fine cloth until completely clear. Wash each eye gently using a clean eye cup. Do this before kajal and before screen time.
  • Foot oiling before sleep: Warm a small amount of sesame oil or coconut oil between your palms and massage the soles of your feet for three to five minutes just before bed. The classical texts say this calms Pitta and helps the eyes recover during sleep.
  • Daily amla: Take one teaspoon of amla powder stirred into a glass of warm water each morning, or drink fresh amla juice. Avoid adding salt or sugar. Consistent daily use over weeks is what the tradition recommends.
  • The 20-second sky pause: Once in the morning and once in the late afternoon, step outside and let your gaze rest on the open sky or a patch of green for 20 seconds without squinting or straining. The classical tradition considers gentle, natural light cooling for the eyes and settling for Alochaka Pitta.
  • Natural kajal discipline: If you use kajal, choose preparations made without synthetic dyes or heavy metals. Wash the eye area clean each morning before applying fresh kajal. Never sleep with old kajal in place.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: Ayurveda has kept eyes sharp for centuries with habits as simple as oiling your feet at night. Here is the classical view on daily eye care, straight from the granthas.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Oil the soles of your feet with warm sesame oil each night: classical texts link this directly to eye health through body channels.
A well-strained Triphala eye wash, made fresh each morning, is one of the simplest classical habits for strengthening vision.
Amla taken daily is Ayurveda's foremost Rasayana for the eyes, cooling Pitta and nourishing visual tissues from within.

glossary_terms.definition: Tiredness or strain of the eyes from accumulated overuse or excess heat.
The sub-dosha of Pitta that governs vision, light perception, and the health of the eye tissues.
The practice of oiling and massaging the feet, particularly the soles, before sleep.
Indian gooseberry (amla): a Rasayana herb considered the primary classical tonic for eye health.
A combination of three fruits (Amalaki, Bibhitaki, Haritaki) used widely in Ayurveda; classically recommended as an eye wash.
Medicated collyrium or kajal applied to the eye rim; a classical daily practice for eye protection and care.

sources: Sushruta Samhita, Uttaratantra, chapters on netra roga (eye disorders)
Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, on the benefits of Abhyanga
Ashtanga Hridaya, Sharira Sthana, on the sub-doshas including Alochaka Pitta
Ashtanga Hridaya, Uttarasthana (Shalakya Tantra), on eye disorders and Triphala eye wash
Bhavaprakasha, Bhavaprakasha Nighantu, entry on Amalaki as Rasayana

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Netra Shrama" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Netra Shrama is defined as: Tiredness or strain of the eyes from accumulated overuse or excess heat.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to triphala eye wash. Steep one level teaspoon of Triphala powder in a cup of room-temperature water overnight. In the morning, strain twice through a fine cloth until completely clear. Wash each eye gently using a clean eye cup. Do this before kajal and before screen time.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to foot oiling before sleep. Warm a small amount of sesame oil or coconut oil between your palms and massage the soles of your feet for three to five minutes just before bed. The classical texts say this calms Pitta and helps the eyes recover during sleep.
